era_context: 1962 sits at the most dangerous inflection point of the Cold War. The superpower confrontation between the United States and Soviet Union dominates global affairs, with nuclear annihilation a real possibility. Decolonisation is accelerating across Africa, with a wave of newly independent states navigating post-colonial instability. China is recovering from the catastrophic Great Leap Forward famine while its rift with the Soviet Union deepens. The global South is contested terrain in the ideological struggle between capitalism and communism, and the Non-Aligned Movement is attempting to carve out a third path.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Central Asia (Soviet-era records underrepresented in anglophone historiography)
Pacific Islands (minimal coverage in global narratives)
Central Africa outside the Congo crisis (sparse coverage of internal developments in Chad, Central African Republic, Gabon)

events.title: Cuban Missile Crisis
events.description: From 16–28 October, the United States and Soviet Union came closer to nuclear war than at any other point in history. U.S. reconnaissance flights discovered Soviet medium-range ballistic missile installations under construction in Cuba. President Kennedy imposed a naval quarantine on Cuba and demanded removal of the missiles. After thirteen days of brinkmanship — during which nuclear-armed Soviet submarines were depth-charged and a U-2 was shot down over Cuba — Khrushchev agreed to withdraw the missiles in exchange for a U.S. pledge not to invade Cuba and the secret removal of American Jupiter missiles from Turkey. The crisis fundamentally altered Cold War dynamics, leading directly to the establishment of the Moscow–Washington hotline and accelerating arms control negotiations.
key_figures: John F. Kennedy (President of the United States)
Nikita Khrushchev (Premier of the Soviet Union)
Anatoly Dobrynin (Soviet Ambassador to the U.S.)
Adlai Stevenson (U.S. Ambassador to the UN)
Vasili Arkhipov (Soviet submarine officer who refused nuclear torpedo launch)

sources.name: Kennedy-Khrushchev correspondence, October 1962 (declassified)
ExComm meeting transcripts (declassified 1996–2001)
Sheldon Stern, The Cuban Missile Crisis in American Memory: Myths versus Reality (2012)
Aleksandr Fursenko and Timothy Naftali, One Hell of a Gamble: Khrushchev, Castro, and Kennedy, 1958–1964 (1997)

events.certainty_note: Extensively documented from all three sides (U.S., Soviet, Cuban) through declassified archives, memoirs, and retrospective conferences involving surviving participants.

events.title: John Glenn Orbits the Earth
events.description: On 20 February, astronaut John Glenn aboard Friendship 7 became the first American to orbit the Earth, completing three orbits in 4 hours and 55 minutes. The mission was a critical milestone in the U.S.-Soviet space race, partially closing the gap opened by Yuri Gagarin's orbital flight the previous year. Glenn's flight restored American confidence in its space programme and cemented public support for the Apollo programme. The mission included a tense re-entry when telemetry falsely suggested the heat shield was loose, a problem resolved by leaving the retropack attached.

sources.name: NASA Mission Report, Mercury-Atlas 6 (1962)
Contemporary reporting, The New York Times, 21 February 1962

events.certainty_note: Fully documented by NASA telemetry, mission transcripts, and global media coverage.

events.title: Algerian Independence — End of the Algerian War
events.description: On 18 March, France and the Provisional Government of the Algerian Republic signed the Evian Accords, ending seven and a half years of brutal war that had killed an estimated 300,000 to 1.5 million Algerians. A referendum on 1 July produced an overwhelming vote for independence, which was proclaimed on 5 July. The war had toppled the French Fourth Republic, brought de Gaulle to power, provoked an attempted military coup and OAS terrorism within France, and caused the exodus of roughly one million pieds-noirs settlers. Algeria's independence was a landmark moment in decolonisation and profoundly influenced anti-colonial movements worldwide.
key_figures: Ahmed Ben Bella (first President of Algeria)
Ferhat Abbas (President of the Provisional Government)

sources.name: Evian Accords, 18 March 1962
Alistair Horne, A Savage War of Peace: Algeria 1954–1962 (1977)
Contemporary reporting, Le Monde, March–July 1962

events.certainty_note: Thoroughly documented in French and Algerian archives, media, and extensive historiography. Casualty figures remain disputed — the range reflects French government and FLN estimates respectively.

events.title: Sino-Indian War
events.description: On 20 October, China launched a massive coordinated assault across the disputed Aksai Chin and North-East Frontier Agency (NEFA) borders, routing unprepared Indian forces. After a month of fighting that resulted in a decisive Chinese military victory, Beijing declared a unilateral ceasefire on 21 November and withdrew to pre-war positions in NEFA while retaining Aksai Chin. The war shattered India's 'Hindi-Chini bhai-bhai' policy and Nehru's prestige, prompted a major Indian military modernisation programme, and ended any pretence of Sino-Indian solidarity within the Non-Aligned Movement. It also pushed India closer to the Soviet Union.
key_figures: Mao Zedong (Chairman, People's Republic of China)
V.K. Krishna Menon (Indian Defence Minister, resigned after the war)

sources.name: Neville Maxwell, India's China War (1970)
Henderson Brooks–Bhagat Report (classified Indian military review, partially leaked 2014)
Official Chinese government statements, October–November 1962

events.certainty_note: Well documented from both sides, though key Indian military records (Henderson Brooks Report) remain officially classified.

events.title: Second Vatican Council Opens
events.description: On 11 October, Pope John XXIII convened the Second Vatican Council (Vatican II), the most significant event in the Catholic Church since the Council of Trent in the sixteenth century. The Council, which would continue in sessions until 1965 under Pope Paul VI, was called to 'open the windows' of the Church to the modern world. Its eventual decrees transformed Catholic practice — introducing vernacular liturgy, affirming religious liberty, engaging in ecumenical dialogue with other Christian churches and non-Christian religions, and redefining the Church's relationship with Judaism. Over 2,600 bishops attended the opening session.
key_figures: Pope John XXIII (convener of the Council)
Augustin Cardinal Bea (president of the Secretariat for Promoting Christian Unity)
Joseph Ratzinger (theological adviser, later Pope Benedict XVI)

sources.name: Apostolic Constitution Humanae Salutis, 25 December 1961, convoking the Council
John W. O'Malley, What Happened at Vatican II (2008)

events.certainty_note: Meticulously documented by the Vatican, participating bishops' diaries, and global media.

events.title: James Meredith Enrolls at the University of Mississippi
events.description: On 1 October, James Meredith became the first African American student admitted to the University of Mississippi, but only after a constitutional crisis. Governor Ross Barnett personally blocked Meredith's enrollment, defying federal court orders. President Kennedy federalised the Mississippi National Guard and dispatched U.S. Marshals. On the night of 30 September, a violent riot erupted on campus: two people were killed, hundreds injured, and 30,000 federal troops were ultimately deployed. The crisis demonstrated both the violent resistance to desegregation in the Deep South and the federal government's willingness to enforce civil rights through military force.
key_figures: James Meredith (student and Air Force veteran)

sources.name: Federal court records, Meredith v. Fair (1962)
Charles Eagles, The Price of Defiance: James Meredith and the Integration of Ole Miss (2009)
Contemporary reporting, The New York Times, October 1962

events.certainty_note: Extensively documented by court records, White House tapes, media, and participant testimony.

events.title: Yemen Civil War Begins
events.description: On 26 September, a military coup led by Colonel Abdullah al-Sallal overthrew the Mutawakkilite Kingdom of Yemen and proclaimed the Yemen Arab Republic. The deposed Imam Muhammad al-Badr escaped to the northern mountains and rallied royalist tribal forces. The resulting civil war became a proxy conflict: Nasser's Egypt deployed up to 70,000 troops supporting the republicans, while Saudi Arabia and Jordan backed the royalists. The war would drag on until 1970, becoming Egypt's 'Vietnam' — draining resources and military capacity that were badly needed during the 1967 war with Israel.
key_figures: Abdullah al-Sallal (leader of the republican coup)
Crown Prince Faisal (Saudi Arabia, supporting royalists)

sources.name: Dana Adams Schmidt, Yemen: The Unknown War (1968)
Jesse Ferris, Nasser's Gamble: How Intervention in Yemen Caused the Six-Day War and the Decline of Egyptian Power (2013)
Contemporary reporting, The Times (London), September–October 1962

events.certainty_note: Well documented through Egyptian, Saudi, and Western diplomatic and journalistic sources.

events.title: Uganda Gains Independence
events.description: On 9 October, Uganda became independent from Britain with Milton Obote as Prime Minister and the Kabaka (king) of Buganda, Mutesa II, as ceremonial president. The new state inherited deep ethnic and regional tensions, particularly between the southern Bantu kingdoms — especially the powerful Buganda — and northern Nilotic groups. This uneasy power-sharing arrangement would collapse within four years as Obote abolished the kingdoms in 1966, setting the stage for decades of political violence culminating in the Amin dictatorship.
key_figures: Iain Macleod (former British Colonial Secretary who shaped the transition)

sources.name: Uganda Independence Act 1962, UK Parliament
Phares Mutibwa, Uganda Since Independence: A Story of Unfulfilled Hopes (1992)

events.certainty_note: Fully documented in British colonial records and Ugandan parliamentary proceedings.

events.title: Jamaica and Trinidad and Tobago Gain Independence
events.description: Jamaica became independent on 6 August and Trinidad and Tobago on 31 August, both from Britain, following the dissolution of the West Indies Federation in 1962. Jamaica's independence came after a referendum in September 1961 in which voters chose to leave the Federation. Alexander Bustamante became Jamaica's first Prime Minister, and Eric Williams became Trinidad's first Prime Minister. These were the first anglophone Caribbean nations to gain independence and set the template for the decolonisation of the remaining British West Indies over the following two decades.
key_figures: Eric Williams (Prime Minister of Trinidad and Tobago)

sources.name: Jamaica Independence Act 1962; Trinidad and Tobago Independence Act 1962, UK Parliament
Eric Williams, History of the People of Trinidad and Tobago (1962)

events.certainty_note: Documented in British parliamentary records and Caribbean governmental archives.

events.title: Rwanda Gains Independence Amid Ethnic Violence
events.description: On 1 July, Rwanda became independent from Belgian trusteeship under a Hutu-dominated republic led by Grégoire Kayibanda. Independence followed the 1959–1961 Hutu revolution, which had overthrown the Tutsi monarchy and triggered the flight of an estimated 120,000–300,000 Tutsi refugees into neighbouring countries. Cross-border raids by Tutsi exile groups ('inyenzi') provoked retaliatory massacres of Tutsi civilians within Rwanda in late 1963. The refugee crisis and political exclusion of the Tutsi population created the conditions that would ultimately lead to the 1994 genocide.
key_figures: Dominique Mbonyumutwa (key figure in the Hutu revolution)

sources.name: René Lemarchand, Rwanda and Burundi (1970)
UN Trusteeship Council records, 1962

events.certainty_note: Documented in Belgian colonial records, UN trusteeship records, and extensive scholarly literature. Casualty and refugee figures are estimates with significant uncertainty.

events.title: Telstar — First Active Communications Satellite
events.description: On 10 July, Telstar 1, built by Bell Telephone Laboratories and launched by NASA, became the first active communications satellite to relay television signals, telephone calls, and data between the United States and Europe. The first transatlantic television transmission occurred on 11 July. Though Telstar 1 operated for only seven months before radiation damage from high-altitude nuclear tests (Starfish Prime) degraded its transistors, it demonstrated the feasibility of satellite communications and launched the industry that would transform global telecommunications.
key_figures: John R. Pierce (Bell Labs engineer, conceptual father of communications satellites)

sources.name: Bell System Technical Journal, special Telstar issue, July 1963
NASA Launch Report, Delta DM-19/Telstar 1, July 1962

events.certainty_note: Fully documented by NASA and AT&T/Bell Labs technical records and global media coverage.

events.title: Starfish Prime — High-Altitude Nuclear Test
events.description: On 9 July, the United States detonated a 1.4-megaton W-49 nuclear warhead at an altitude of 400 km above Johnston Atoll in the Pacific, as part of Operation Fishbowl. The resulting electromagnetic pulse (EMP) knocked out street lights and telephone infrastructure in Hawaii, 1,400 km away, and created an artificial radiation belt that damaged or destroyed at least six satellites over the following months, including Telstar 1. The test provided critical data on the effects of nuclear detonations in space and EMP, and contributed to the momentum for the 1963 Partial Nuclear Test Ban Treaty.
key_figures: James Van Allen (physicist who warned about radiation belt effects)

sources.name: Defense Nuclear Agency, Operation Dominic I: 1962 (DNA 6040F, declassified)
Contemporary scientific reports, Journal of Geophysical Research, 1963

events.certainty_note: Documented by U.S. defense and scientific records. Some technical details remained classified for decades.

events.title: Rachel Carson Publishes Silent Spring
events.description: Rachel Carson's Silent Spring was published on 27 September, documenting the devastating ecological effects of indiscriminate pesticide use, particularly DDT. Serialised in The New Yorker beginning in June, the book provoked a furious counterattack from the chemical industry but also a massive public response. President Kennedy's Science Advisory Committee investigated and vindicated Carson's core claims in May 1963. The book is widely credited as a catalyst for the modern environmental movement and ultimately led to the creation of the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency in 1970 and the domestic ban on DDT in 1972.
key_figures: John F. Kennedy (President, ordered PSAC investigation)

sources.name: Rachel Carson, Silent Spring (Houghton Mifflin, 1962)
President's Science Advisory Committee, Use of Pesticides (May 1963)
Mark Hamilton Lytle, The Gentle Subversive: Rachel Carson, Silent Spring, and the Rise of the Environmental Movement (2007)

events.certainty_note: Publication date, content, and public impact are fully documented.

events.title: Nelson Mandela Arrested
events.description: On 5 August, Nelson Mandela was arrested at a police roadblock near Howick, Natal, after seventeen months underground organising Umkhonto we Sizwe (MK), the armed wing of the ANC. He had secretly travelled abroad earlier in 1962 to secure support and military training for MK. He was tried and convicted of incitement and leaving the country illegally, receiving a five-year sentence in November. This arrest led to the discovery of the MK leadership at Liliesleaf Farm in Rivonia in 1963, setting the stage for the Rivonia Trial that would sentence Mandela to life imprisonment.
key_figures: Cecil Williams (MK associate, was driving when arrested)
Walter Sisulu (ANC leader, arrested later at Rivonia)

sources.name: Nelson Mandela, Long Walk to Freedom (1994)
South African court records, State v. Mandela (1962)
Tom Lodge, Mandela: A Critical Life (2006)

events.certainty_note: Documented in court records, police files, and Mandela's own autobiography. There is credible evidence, reported by the South African press and later confirmed by a retired CIA officer, that the CIA tipped off the South African police about Mandela's whereabouts.

events.title: Western Samoa Becomes First Independent Pacific Island State
events.description: On 1 January, Western Samoa (now Samoa) became the first small Pacific island nation to gain independence in the twentieth century, ending a UN trusteeship administered by New Zealand since 1920. Independence was led by Fiame Mata'afa Faumuina Mulinu'u II as Prime Minister under a constitutional system blending Westminster parliamentary democracy with the fa'amatai (chiefly) system. Suffrage was initially restricted to matai (chiefs). Western Samoa's independence served as a model and inspiration for other Pacific island decolonisation movements.
key_figures: Fiame Mata'afa Faumuina Mulinu'u II (Prime Minister)

sources.name: UN General Assembly Resolution 1626 (XVI), 18 October 1961, approving Samoan independence
Malama Meleisea, The Making of Modern Samoa (1987)

events.certainty_note: Documented in UN records and New Zealand colonial archives.

events.title: Thalidomide Crisis and Drug Safety Reform
events.description: The thalidomide disaster reached its full public impact in 1962, as thousands of children were born worldwide with severe limb deformities (phocomelia) caused by the sedative drug thalidomide, prescribed to pregnant women for morning sickness since 1957. The drug had been marketed by the West German firm Chemie Grünenthal. The crisis led directly to the Kefauver-Harris Amendment to the U.S. Federal Food, Drug, and Cosmetic Act, signed 10 October 1962, which for the first time required drug manufacturers to prove efficacy and safety before marketing. FDA reviewer Frances Oldham Kelsey, who had blocked the drug's approval in the U.S., was awarded the President's Award for Distinguished Federal Civilian Service.
key_figures: Frances Oldham Kelsey (FDA reviewer who blocked U.S. approval)
Widukind Lenz (German geneticist who identified the causal link)
William McBride (Australian physician who independently identified the link)

sources.name: Kefauver-Harris Amendment (Drug Efficacy Amendment), Public Law 87-781, 10 October 1962
Widukind Lenz, 'Thalidomide and Congenital Abnormalities,' The Lancet, January 1962
Trent Stephens and Rock Brynner, Dark Remedy: The Impact of Thalidomide and Its Revival as a Vital Medicine (2001)

events.certainty_note: Medical and legislative records provide comprehensive documentation. Total number of affected children remains uncertain — estimates range from 10,000 to 20,000 worldwide.

events.title: Burmese Military Coup
events.description: On 2 March, General Ne Win seized power in a bloodless military coup, overthrowing the democratically elected government of Prime Minister U Nu. Ne Win established a Revolutionary Council, dissolved parliament, and announced 'The Burmese Way to Socialism,' an idiosyncratic programme combining Marxist economics with Buddhist philosophy and Burmese nationalism. The regime nationalised industry, expelled foreign businesses, and isolated Burma from the international community. On 7 July, troops fired on student protesters at Rangoon University, killing at least 15, and the Student Union building was dynamited. This coup inaugurated military rule that would persist in various forms until 2011.
key_figures: General Ne Win (coup leader, chairman of the Revolutionary Council)
Aung San Suu Kyi (daughter of independence hero, then a teenager — would later lead the democracy movement)

sources.name: Robert Taylor, The State in Myanmar (2009)
Revolutionary Council, 'The Burmese Way to Socialism' (policy declaration, April 1962)

events.certainty_note: Well documented in Burmese and international sources. Exact death toll at Rangoon University varies by source.

events.title: French-Algerian OAS Terror Campaign and Its Collapse
events.description: The Organisation armée secrète (OAS), a clandestine paramilitary group of French settlers and dissident military officers opposed to Algerian independence, conducted a campaign of bombings and assassinations throughout 1962 in both Algeria and metropolitan France. The OAS carried out scorched-earth attacks on Algerian infrastructure, including the burning of the University of Algiers library on 7 June. On 22 August, an OAS-linked assassination attempt at Petit-Clamart nearly killed President de Gaulle. The campaign ultimately failed to prevent independence and the OAS leadership was arrested or fled, but the violence accelerated the pieds-noirs exodus.
key_figures: Jean Bastien-Thiry (leader of the Petit-Clamart assassination attempt, later executed)

sources.name: Alexander Harrison, Challenging De Gaulle: The OAS and the Counterrevolution in Algeria (1989)
French judicial records, trial of Bastien-Thiry (1963)

events.certainty_note: Documented in French judicial records, police archives, and extensive journalism.

events.title: Burundi Gains Independence
events.description: On 1 July, Burundi became independent from Belgian trusteeship as a constitutional monarchy under Mwami (King) Mwambutsa IV, with Prince Louis Rwagasore's UPRONA party dominant. Rwagasore himself had been assassinated in October 1961, and his death removed the one leader who might have bridged the Hutu-Tutsi divide. Burundi's post-independence politics were immediately marked by ethnic tension and instability, with a Tutsi-dominated military and civil service ruling over a Hutu majority — a mirror image of neighbouring Rwanda's political dynamic.

sources.name: UN Trusteeship Council records, 1962
René Lemarchand, Rwanda and Burundi (1970)

events.certainty_note: Documented in Belgian colonial and UN trusteeship records.

events.title: Mariner 2 — First Successful Planetary Flyby
events.description: On 14 December, NASA's Mariner 2 spacecraft performed the first successful flyby of another planet, passing within 34,773 km of Venus. The probe confirmed that Venus had extremely high surface temperatures (approximately 425°C), a dense carbon dioxide atmosphere, and no detectable magnetic field — findings that overturned earlier speculation about Venus as a potentially habitable world. Mariner 2 represented the first successful interplanetary mission by any nation and inaugurated the era of planetary exploration.

sources.name: NASA/JPL, Mariner–Venus 1962: Final Project Report (NASA SP-59, 1965)

events.certainty_note: Fully documented by NASA/JPL telemetry and mission reports.

events.title: Sino-Soviet Split Deepens — Public Polemics Begin
events.description: The ideological rift between the Soviet Union and China became increasingly public in 1962. During the Cuban Missile Crisis, Beijing accused Moscow of both 'adventurism' (placing the missiles) and 'capitulationism' (removing them). The Soviets supported India during the Sino-Indian War, further infuriating Beijing. Chinese media began publishing thinly veiled attacks on Soviet 'revisionism.' The split shattered the myth of monolithic international communism, reshaped Cold War alignments globally, and would eventually open the door for the U.S.-China rapprochement of the 1970s.
key_figures: Deng Xiaoping (CCP General Secretary, led Chinese side in ideological disputes)

sources.name: Lorenz Lüthi, The Sino-Soviet Split: Cold War in the Communist World (2008)
People's Daily and Red Flag editorials, 1962 (collected in The Polemic on the General Line of the International Communist Movement, 1965)

events.certainty_note: Documented through party publications, diplomatic records, and declassified archives from both sides.

events.title: Great Leap Forward Famine Ends — China Begins Recovery
events.description: By 1962, the Great Chinese Famine — caused by Mao's Great Leap Forward policies of forced collectivisation, backyard steel production, and grain over-reporting — was subsiding as the regime retreated from its most radical policies. The famine killed an estimated 15–55 million people between 1959 and 1962, making it the deadliest famine in human history. At the January 1962 'Seven Thousand Cadres Conference,' Liu Shaoqi implicitly criticised the Great Leap, calling the disaster '70% man-made, 30% natural.' Mao tactically retreated, allowing Liu and Deng Xiaoping to lead economic recovery, but harboured resentment that would fuel the Cultural Revolution.

sources.name: Frank Dikötter, Mao's Great Famine: The History of China's Most Devastating Catastrophe, 1958–1962 (2010)
Yang Jisheng, Tombstone: The Great Chinese Famine, 1958–1962 (2008/2012 English translation)
Seven Thousand Cadres Conference proceedings, January 1962 (partially available through Chinese party history publications)

events.certainty_note: The famine itself is confirmed beyond doubt; death toll estimates vary enormously (15–55 million) depending on methodology and access to provincial records. The 'Seven Thousand Cadres' conference is documented in party records.

events.title: Brazil Wins Second Consecutive FIFA World Cup
events.description: Brazil won the FIFA World Cup in Chile on 17 June, defeating Czechoslovakia 3–1 in the final. Despite losing Pelé to injury in the group stage, Brazil triumphed through the brilliance of Garrincha, who was the tournament's outstanding player. This was Brazil's second consecutive World Cup victory, cementing the country's status as the dominant force in world football. The tournament was notable for the 'Battle of Santiago' — a notoriously violent match between Chile and Italy — and for being the first World Cup widely broadcast on television in Latin America.
key_figures: Garrincha (Brazilian forward, player of the tournament)

sources.name: FIFA official records, 1962 World Cup
Contemporary press coverage, O Globo, El Mercurio, June 1962

events.certainty_note: Fully documented by FIFA records, television footage, and global media coverage.

events.title: India's Third General Election
events.description: India held its third general election in February, returning the Indian National Congress under Nehru to power with a reduced but still overwhelming majority. The election was notable for significant gains by the Communist Party of India, particularly in Kerala, and by regional parties. The Congress won 361 of 494 Lok Sabha seats on 44.7% of the vote. This was the last election before the Sino-Indian War shattered Nehru's prestige and the last general election Nehru would contest before his death in 1964.

sources.name: Election Commission of India, Report on the Third General Elections in India, 1962
Ramachandra Guha, India After Gandhi (2007)

events.certainty_note: Documented in official Election Commission records.

disconfirming_evidence: Several commonly cited claims about 1962 merit qualification: (1) The Cuban Missile Crisis is often presented as a case of Kennedy's firm resolve forcing a Soviet capitulation. Declassified records from the 1990s–2000s reveal the resolution involved significant American concessions — particularly the secret agreement to remove Jupiter missiles from Turkey — and that Kennedy's inner circle was far more divided than the initial triumphalist narrative suggested. The crisis was resolved through mutual compromise, not one-sided brinkmanship. (2) Vasili Arkhipov's role in 'preventing nuclear war' during the crisis, while genuinely significant, has been amplified in popular accounts beyond what the documentary record fully supports — the exact sequence of events aboard submarine B-59 remains debated among naval historians. (3) The Sino-Indian War is often attributed primarily to Chinese aggression, but Neville Maxwell's India's China War (1970) and the leaked Henderson Brooks Report argue that India's 'Forward Policy' of establishing outposts in disputed territory was a significant provocation — a view contested by Indian nationalists but supported by much of the archival evidence. (4) Rachel Carson's Silent Spring is sometimes credited as single-handedly launching the environmental movement; in reality, it built on decades of prior ecological science and conservation advocacy, and the DDT ban was a long legislative process involving many actors. (5) The OAS assassination attempt at Petit-Clamart is sometimes dramatised as a near-miss that nearly changed history; while genuinely dangerous, some accounts exaggerate how close de Gaulle came to death. (6) CIA involvement in Mandela's arrest was long denied but was effectively confirmed by a retired CIA officer's 1990 account to the press; the extent and nature of the intelligence sharing remains disputed.
historiographic_note: 1962 is one of the most thoroughly documented years in human history, with extensive archives from all major governments (many now declassified), robust independent journalism, television coverage, and satellite monitoring. Key biases include: Cold War framing that privileges the U.S.-Soviet axis over events in the Global South; Western media concentration on European and North American events at the expense of African and Asian developments; and the tendency of national historiographies to present self-serving accounts (e.g., Indian vs. Chinese narratives of the border war, American triumphalism about the Missile Crisis). African independence movements are well documented at the political level but much less so regarding social and economic conditions of ordinary people. The records for internal Chinese politics during this period long depended on fragmentary information; the situation has improved significantly since the 2000s through provincial archive access and the work of historians like Dikötter and Yang Jisheng, though PRC archives remain selectively accessible.

graph_edges.note: Cuban Missile Crisis created urgency for arms control, leading directly to the Partial Nuclear Test Ban Treaty
Soviet missile deployment was partly motivated by desire to protect Cuba after the Bay of Pigs invasion
The Evian Accords ended the war that began in November 1954
Indian military humiliation in 1962 prompted massive rearmament that shifted the regional balance of power
Egyptian overextension in Yemen weakened military capacity before the Six-Day War
The Tutsi refugee crisis created by 1959–1962 events formed the basis for the RPF invasion and subsequent genocide
Silent Spring catalysed the environmental movement that led to the EPA's creation
Mandela's arrest led to the discovery of MK headquarters and the Rivonia Trial
Ne Win's coup inaugurated decades of military rule that provoked the 1988 democratic uprising
The Sino-Soviet Split created the strategic opening for U.S.-China rapprochement
Mao's resentment at being sidelined during the recovery from the famine was a proximate cause of the Cultural Revolution
Starfish Prime's radiation belt damaged Telstar 1, directly linking the nuclear test to the communications satellite
Meredith's enrollment was an enforcement case following the Brown v. Board desegregation ruling

_meta.note: Produced via Claude Code CLI (Max subscription) before API migration
